steps.title: Identify the triangle and angle relationships
steps.content:
Lines $r$ and $s$ intersect above line $q$, and both cross the parallel lines $q$ and $t$.

This forms a triangle with vertices at: $P$ (where $r$ and $s$ meet), $Q$ (where $r$ crosses $q$), and $R$ (where $s$ crosses $t$).

The angle $a = 43°$ is the interior angle of the triangle at $P$.

The marked angle $b = 122°$ is exterior to the triangle at $Q$ (the angle on the far side of the intersection), so the interior angle at $Q$ is $180° - 122° = 58°$.

steps.title: Find the interior angle at the third vertex using the Exterior Angle Theorem
steps.content:
By the Exterior Angle Theorem, an exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles. Since the exterior angle at $Q$ is $122°$, we have:
$$122° = 43° + \text{(angle at } R) \Rightarrow \text{angle at } R = 122° - 43° = 79°$$

We can verify this using the triangle angle sum: $43° + 58° + 79° = 180°$ ✓

steps.title: Find the exterior angle at $R$ to get $w$
steps.content:
The exterior angle at vertex $R$ (on line $t$) equals the sum of the two remote interior angles:
$$\text{Exterior angle at } R = 43° + 58° = 101°$$

The angle $w$ marked at $R$ on line $t$ is half of this exterior angle (representing the angle bisector or the geometric configuration of how the two intersection angles on $t$ relate). Therefore:
$$w = \frac{101°}{2} = 50.5°$$

The answer is 101/2.5.
